skill.name: labarchive-integration
skill.description: Integrate with LabArchives electronic lab notebooks through API examples and Python scripts for authentication, backups, entries, attachments, and research tool workflows.
skill.summary: Automate LabArchives notebook access, backups, entries, attachments, and research integrations.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high malware indicators, but review found no malicious intent, prompt injection attempt, ransomware behavior, command execution, or command and control endpoint. The remaining risk is medium because the skill intentionally handles LabArchives credentials, network API calls, local file uploads, and notebook backup files containing sensitive research data.

medium_findings.title: Credential Storage and Transmission Require Care
medium_findings.description: The setup script collects institutional API credentials and a user external applications password, then writes them to config.yaml with restrictive permissions. The operation is legitimate, but misuse of the config file or a changed API URL could expose LabArchives secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly collect, store, load, and transmit LabArchives credentials. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it creates a clear secret-handling risk if users configure it poorly.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Notebook Data Can Be Uploaded or Exported
medium_findings.description: The entry script uploads user-selected files and entire directories to LabArchives, while the notebook script writes full notebook backups locally. These operations match the advertised purpose but can move regulated or confidential research data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file upload and backup behavior is explicit in the executable scripts. The behavior is legitimate, but users must control paths, permissions, and data classification.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are fenced or inline Markdown examples in documentation. I did not find evidence that Ruby backticks or dynamic shell execution are used by the Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code fences or inline code examples, not executable Ruby backtick calls. The executable Python files do not contain subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec patterns.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to LabArchives regional API endpoints or the documented labarchives-py installation source. These are expected dependencies for a LabArchives integration, not command and control infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are named LabArchives regional API URLs or a GitHub install URL for the Python wrapper. No unrelated or hidden network destination was found.

low_findings.title: Ransomware and Weak Crypto Keyword Hits Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The ransomware keyword hit appears in advice to encrypt configuration files at rest. Weak cryptography hits are associated with words such as API key or authentication documentation, not cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited security text recommends OAuth and secure credential storage. I found no file encryption loop, ransom note, destructive behavior, or weak crypto function use in executable code.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Reflect Local Validation and Backup Writes
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are ordinary config creation, file existence checks, file size display, and notebook backup writes. These operations are expected, but users should avoid sensitive output directories and protect backups.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and easy to inspect. They support configuration, upload validation, and backups rather than hidden discovery or destructive file changes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing HTTP Upload to Configured API URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The upload workflow builds a URL from config.yaml and sends access_key_id and access_password with a multipart file upload. This is required for the API workflow but should be restricted to trusted LabArchives endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request data includes both credential fields and is sent with requests.post. The destination is configurable, so endpoint trust matters.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Credential Configuration File Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup script writes API credentials and user credentials to config.yaml, then applies mode 600. This is reasonable but still creates a local secret file that users must protect.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script writes the collected configuration to disk and sets restrictive permissions. The mitigation is present, but the secret-at-rest risk remains.

content.value_statement: Research teams need reliable ways to move data into and out of LabArchives notebooks. This skill provides API guidance and scripts for authentication, backups, entries, attachments, and integrations.
actual_capabilities: Configure LabArchives regional API endpoints and credential files.
Authenticate users and retrieve user IDs for API workflows.
List accessible notebooks and create local notebook backups.
Create notebook entries, comments, and file attachments.
Batch upload files from a local directory to an existing entry.
Guide integrations with Jupyter, REDCap, Protocols.io, GraphPad Prism, SnapGene, and Geneious.
limitations: Requires a LabArchives Enterprise account with API access enabled.
Requires users to supply valid institutional and user credentials.
Does not validate compliance requirements for regulated data.
Depends on LabArchives API behavior and the labarchives-py client package.

use_cases.title: Upload Experiment Outputs
use_cases.description: Create entries and attach result files after experiments without manually re-entering metadata.

use_cases.title: Back Up Lab Notebooks
use_cases.description: Export notebooks with timestamps for retention, review, and disaster recovery workflows.

use_cases.title: Connect Analysis Tools
use_cases.description: Plan workflows that move Jupyter, REDCap, Protocols.io, or analysis files into LabArchives records.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure LabArchives API access for the US endpoint and explain what credentials I need.

prompt_templates.title: List Notebooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to list my accessible LabArchives notebooks and identify the notebook ID for a project.

prompt_templates.title: Back Up Notebook
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me back up LabArchives notebook NOTEBOOK_ID with attachments and store it in a local backups folder.

prompt_templates.title: Design Integration Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that exports a completed Jupyter analysis into LabArchives with the notebook file, HTML summary, and metadata comment.

output: The workflow authenticates with LabArchives and requests a full backup for notebook 67890.
The backup is saved in the backups folder with a timestamped archive name.
The response includes the saved file path and file size for verification.

output_examples.input: Upload my results file to an existing entry.
output: The workflow checks that the selected file exists before upload.
It sends the file to the selected LabArchives notebook entry using the configured API endpoint.
It reports whether the upload succeeded and shows the HTTP status when it fails.

output_examples.input: Plan a Jupyter to LabArchives export.
output: The plan converts the notebook to an HTML record for reading in LabArchives.
It attaches the original notebook file for reproducibility.
It adds a metadata comment with execution time and environment details.

best_practices: Store config.yaml outside source control and restrict access to trusted users.
Use the correct regional LabArchives endpoint for the institution.
Test uploads and backups in a noncritical notebook before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not enter credentials into an untrusted custom API endpoint.
Do not batch upload directories before checking their contents.
Do not store notebook backups in shared folders without access controls.

faq.question: What credentials does this skill need?
faq.answer: It needs institutional LabArchives API credentials and a user external applications password.

faq.question: Can it work without Enterprise API access?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows require LabArchives API access enabled by the institution.

faq.question: Does it upload files automatically?
faq.answer: No. The scripts upload files only when the user runs the upload or batch upload commands.

faq.question: Where are backups stored?
faq.answer: Backups are written to a local output folder, using timestamped filenames generated by the script.

faq.question: Does it support Jupyter workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The references describe exporting Jupyter notebooks to LabArchives entries with attachments.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use institutional data policies, trusted endpoints, protected config files, and access-controlled backup locations.
